one_line_takeaway: A taxonomy survey (not an empirical or attack/defense paper) that decomposes split learning for LLM fine-tuning into a unified 11-step pipeline and maps existing model-, system-, and privacy/security-level techniques onto it, arguing that several conventional split-learning optimizations and defenses break down or fail to transfer to LLMs due to memory blow-up on activation concatenation, incompatibility of activation-mixup with autoregressive generation, and the constant per-layer hidden dimension of Transformers negating split-point-based communication savings.
key_finding: The survey's central analytical finding (its own synthesis, not a cited result) is that several classes of split-learning optimization and defense techniques designed for conventional (small, non-LLM) models do not transfer cleanly to LLM fine-tuning, and the authors identify specific failure mechanisms rather than just listing gaps. Examples the paper states directly: (1) feature-merging/concatenation methods for mitigating non-IID data (e.g., MergeSFL, SCALA) 'introduce severe memory bottlenecks' when applied to native LLMs because concatenating smashed data from multiple clients 'rapidly exhausts server GPU capacity, triggering catastrophic out-of-memory failures'; (2) smashed-data mixup methods (e.g., Mix2SFL) are 'fundamentally incompatible with natural language generation (NLG) tasks, as interpolating variable-length hidden states and shifted next-token labels destroys semantic integrity and triggers convergence collapse'; (3) unlike CNNs, Transformer blocks have a constant hidden dimension across layers, so shifting the split point does not change the communication payload size, meaning split-point selection alone cannot reduce LLM communication overhead and dedicated compression is required; (4) because LLM supervised fine-tuning uses teacher forcing (labels = input sequence shifted by one token), label leakage in U-shaped split learning is asserted to be 'mathematically equivalent to exposing raw proprietary text,' which the authors argue prior (non-LLM) surveys overlooked as a distinct, amplified privacy risk.
core_contribution: This is a survey paper (Liu, Wang, Wang, Tang, Wu, Zhejiang University), not an empirical or attack/defense paper. Its own contribution, as stated by the authors, is threefold: (1) a 'unified split-based LLM-FT pipeline' that decomposes split learning for LLM fine-tuning into 11 granular operational steps (model partitioning; client/server forward and backward propagation; activation and gradient transmission; federated aggregation), shared across Vanilla (two-part) and U-shaped (three-part) split learning and across framework variants (Sequential SL, Parallel SL, Split Federated Learning, Group-based SFL); (2) a 'multi-dimensional bottleneck analysis' that maps model-level, system-level, and privacy/security vulnerabilities directly onto specific steps of this pipeline; and (3) a 'component-anchored literature taxonomy' organizing existing split-learning-for-LLM work (model optimization, system efficiency, and privacy attacks/defenses) against the pipeline steps they target. The paper explicitly frames itself as the first survey dedicated specifically to split learning applied to LLM fine-tuning (as opposed to prior surveys of split learning for conventional/small models), and it summarizes cited work in tables (Table 2: model-level optimization methods; Table 3: global joint-optimization methods; Table 4: smashed-data inversion attacks) rather than presenting new experiments, new attacks, new defenses, or new empirical measurements of its own.
threat_model: Not applicable in the usual sense: the paper does not propose or evaluate a single threat model of its own. Instead, as part of its survey scope (Section 5), it catalogs the threat models used across the split-learning attack literature it reviews: (a) an honest-but-curious/passive server that observes transmitted smashed data (forward activations) and gradients and tries to reconstruct client inputs or labels without deviating from protocol (e.g., UnSplit, GLASS/GLASS++, PCAT-family, MAERA, FSA, FORA, SDAR); (b) an active/malicious server that deviates from protocol -- e.g., Feature-Space Hijacking Attack (FSHA), SIA, SplitSpy -- by injecting forged gradients from an internal discriminator to coerce the client model into an invertible feature space, then decoding smashed data to recover raw inputs; (c) gradient-based label-reconstruction adversaries exploiting that in causal language modeling the label is just the input sequence shifted by one token, so label leakage is described as mathematically equivalent to leaking raw text (metric-based, optimization-based/gradient-matching, and learning-based/model-completion sub-variants, e.g., DLG/iDLG/TAG/LAMP, ExPLoit, Chen et al.'s semi-white-box surrogate approach); (d) bidirectional attacks (e.g., BiSR) combining forward smashed-data inversion and backward gradient matching; and (e) model-manipulation adversaries who exploit server control over the trunk model to run data-poisoning or backdoor attacks against split-based LLM fine-tuning. The survey does not adjudicate which threat model is most realistic for production split-LLM-FT deployments; it presents them as a taxonomy (Fig. 6) organized by data-reconstruction vs. model-manipulation attack families.
limitations: As a survey, the paper's own stated novelty claims should be read against its cited comparison table (Table 1): several prior surveys already cover subsets of the same territory (e.g., attack/defense summaries in refs [62],[120]; resource-efficiency/system optimization in [78]; perturbation vs. learning-based defense evaluation in [41]), and this paper's differentiation rests on combining LLM-specificity with a unified pipeline decomposition, not on covering fundamentally new attack/defense primitives. The 'LLM Exp.' column in Table 2 shows that most model-level optimization methods listed were never empirically validated on LLMs by their original authors -- the paper's applicability judgments (NLU/NLG) for those methods are explicitly labeled as the survey authors' own theoretical projections rather than demonstrated results, which is an important caveat when using this survey to infer what actually works for LLM fine-tuning. The paper also has bibliographic/production artifacts visible in the paper (e.g., an ACM template placeholder conference name, a copyright year of 2018 that is inconsistent with citations through 2026, and an arXiv identifier of 2604.24468 with a 27 Apr 2026 timestamp), consistent with a preprint/camera-ready draft rather than a final, cleanly formatted publication -- these are metadata issues, not substantive flaws in the survey's content.
